content.full_text:
RAvI MEHTA
C H A RMAN
FAIR POLITICAL ‘PRACTICES
COMMISSION
April 8,
1996
Daniel M.
Kolkey
Counsel to the Governor
and Legal Affairs Secretary
Governor’s Office
Sacramento, California
95814
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A-96—101
Dear Mr.
Kolkey:
This is in response to your request for advice regarding the
statement of economic interest disclosure requirements of the
Political Reform Act
(the “Act”).
1
QUESTION
The California Judicial Council reimburses your travel
expenses incurred in attending meetings of the Council’s Blue
Ribbon Commission on Jury System Improvement.
Are these
reimbursements reportable on your next year’s statement of
economic interests?
CONCLUSION
No.
Because the Judicial Council is. considered a state
agency,
the reimbursements are exempt from the definition of
income under Section 82030(b) (2)
of the -Act.
FACTS
You are the Governor’s Legal Affairs Secretary,
and in that
capacity,
you have been appointed to the Judicial Council’s Blue
Ribbon Commission on Jury System Improvement,
which is charged
with making recommendations to the Council for improving the jury
system.
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations,
Sections
18000—18995.
428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814
(916) 322-5660


File No.
A—96—lOl
Page
2
The Judicial Council was established under Article VI,
section
6 of the California Constitution and consists of judges,
members of the State Bar,
and one member of each house of the
Legislature.
The Council makes recommendations to the courts and
to the Governor and Legislature to improve the administration of
justice,
and adopts rules for court administration, practice and
procedure not inconsistent with statute.
At the Blue Ribbon Commission’s meetings,
commission members
review and discuss various proposals with a view toward developing
recommendations for the Judicial Council.
ANALYSIS
Under the Act,
payments for travel that are provided to a
public official by a third party are generally considered income
if the official provided equal or greater consideration for the
receipt of the payments,
or gifts if the official did not provide
equal or greater consideration for such payments.
You serve on the Judicial Council’s Blue Ribbon Commission on
Jury System Improvement.
The Commission is charged with making
recommendations to the Judicial Council for improving the jury
system.
As a member of the Commission,
you review and discuss
various proposals regarding changes to the jury system and develop
recommendations for the Judicial Council.
Under these
circumstances, it appears that you are providing consLderation for
the travel reimbursements such that they would be considered
income to you,
not a gift.
2
Under Section 82030 of the Act “income” is defined to include
any salary,
wage,
advance,
any gift of food or beverage,
reimbursement for expenses,
and per diem,
among others.
However,
Section 82030(b) (2)
provides that income does not include the
following:
Salary and reimbursement for expenses or per diem
received from a state,
local, or federal government
agency and reimbursement for travel expenses and per
diem received from a bona fide educational,
academic,
or
charitable organization.
(Emphasis added.)
2
Note that in order for the travel payments to be considered
income, the official must be able to demonstrate that he or she
has provided consideration of equal or greater value in return for
the payments.
(Benninghoven Advice Letter,
No.
1-93-298; Riddle
Advice Letter,
No.
A—89—200;
and Quan Advice Letter,
No.
A-89-
182.)


File No.
A—96--l0l
Page
3
Under Section 82049,
a “state agency” means every state
office,
department, division, bureau,
board and commission,
and
the Legislature.
For purposes of the Act, the Judicial Council is
considered a state government agency.
The Judicial Council has a
conflict of interest code.
(See Gampell Advice Letter,
No.
A-85-
211.)
Section 82011(e)
of the Act provides that the Chief Justice
or his or her designee is the code reviewing body with respect to
the conflict of interest code of the Judicial Council.
Further,
the Judicial Council is established by the State Constitution and
funded by the state.
Therefore,
the reimbursements provided to you by the Judicial
Council for travel costs you incur attending meetings of the
Council’s Blue Ribbon Commission on Jury System Improvement would
be exempt from the definition of income under the Act.
Accordingly,
you are not required to report such payments on your
next year’s statement of economic interests.
I trust this answers your question.
If you have any further
questions regarding this matter, please contact me at 916/322-
5660.
Sincerely,
Steven G.
Churchwell
General Counsel
P
By:
Hyla P.
Wagner
Counsel,
Legal Division
SGC:HPW:a]c

of the -Act.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: The California Judicial Council reimburses your travel
expenses incurred in attending meetings of the Council’s Blue
Ribbon Commission on Jury System Improvement.
Are these
reimbursements reportable on your next year’s statement of
economic interests?

CONCLUSION: No.
Because the Judicial Council is. considered a state
agency,
the reimbursements are exempt from the definition of
income under Section 82030(b) (2)
of the -Act.
